title: Judging Addicts: Drug Courts and Coercion in the Justice System By Rebecca Tiger NYU Press. 2012. 208 pages. $23 paper
abstract: Specialized drug courts have been part of the criminal justice landscape in the United States for nearly a quarter of a century. While operational specifics vary from one jurisdiction to the next, drug courts offer qualifying defendants a regime of supervised treatment in lieu of traditional forms of adjudication. Drug court participants engage in traditional forms of substance abuse treatment (ranging from inpatient hospitalization to residential treatment, outpatient services, 12-step support groups, and so forth) under the watchful gaze of court officials. Participants periodically return to court to appear before a judge who has the authority to reward and to sanction. Failure to engage in one's treatment program can lead to sanctions (including jail time) which, theoretically, helps drug court participants remain in treatment through its most difficult phases. The result, advocates claim, is an “innovation” in criminal justice that radically intercedes in the cycle of addiction and crime by effectively harnessing the power of both punishment and therapy.
